title: Most freight brokers are scaling the wrong thing.
whyItWorks: Directly names the 86-89% pilot failure rate from briefing intel. Freight brokers default to adding headcount instead of building agent-first workflows. This reframes 'scaling' as an ops design problem, not a hiring problem.

linkedin.hook: Most freight brokers are scaling the wrong thing.
linkedin.format: text post with screenshot of pilot-failure stat + on-camera reel
linkedin.description: Lead with the 86% pilot failure number, then pivot to what 'scaling' actually looks like in 2026. Mention BCAT Logistics headcount vs. output ratio and JobsDone Labs integrations naturally.
linkedin.caption:
Most freight brokers are scaling the wrong thing.

86% of enterprise AI agent pilots fail.
But for the freight world, that stat isn't about tech. It's about ops.

We kept adding people — dispatchers, carrier reps, operations managers — while our quote-to-book cycle stayed stuck at 48 hours.

Scaling headcount is easy. Scaling speed is hard.

The brokers winning 2026 aren't the ones with the biggest team.
They're the ones who stopped doing dumb shit manually.

Lead scoring. Carrier vetting. Auto-follow-up on open RFQs.

The tool stack replaces the busywork. Your reps close the lanes.

If your gross profit is flat and headcount keeps growing, you're scaling the wrong thing.

DM me 'SCALE' if you want to see what our agent stack handles for BCAT.
linkedin.aiCreativePrompt: Background: dark #0B0D12 dashboard with cream #F4EFE3 text. Left side shows org-chart with red X on headcount icon. Right side shows #1466FF agent stack icons with green checkmarks. Slow zoom to the agent stack.
scriptSparknotes: Opener: 'Most freight brokers are scaling the wrong thing.'
Cut to BCAT dashboard showing flat headcount vs rising shipments.
Name the three agent workflows: scoring, vetting, follow-up.
